topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the office hummed, a grating soundtrack to Clara’s mounting frustration. She slammed the file folder onto her desk, the sound sharp enough to make Mark flinch. "Are you even listening to me, Mark?" she snapped, her voice tight. She stared at the shared calendar on the monitor, her jaw clenched so tightly her teeth ached. A small, faded photograph of a muddy lake surrounded by pine trees stared back at her. It was a picture from that summer. "I swear, if you’ve been talking about that cabin again, I’m going to lose it." Mark just shrugged, a careless ease in his posture that only fueled her simmering annoyance. "It was a good time, Clara. You should try to relax." The suggestion felt like a deliberate slight. She pushed back from her desk, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor. "Relax? I haven't been able to focus all week because I keep thinking about that absolute disaster of a summer camp."

[story 2]

Liam felt a hot flush creep up his neck as he reread the old camp brochure. Every glossy image of canoes and campfire songs seemed to mock his current predicament. He rubbed his temples, the pressure behind his eyes building. He looked over at Sarah, who was laughing at something on her phone, oblivious to his internal turmoil. "Did you see that?" Liam finally managed, his tone clipped and sharp. "The same lake. The same counselor's name." Sarah looked up, a flicker of confusion crossing her face. "What are you talking about, Liam?" He threw the brochure onto the table, the paper fluttering like a defeated flag. "Don't play dumb with me, Sarah. We went to Camp Whispering Pines together. I distinctly remember the smell of pine needles and cheap bug spray. It’s ridiculous that we share that memory, and now this."

[story 3]

Maria paced the length of her cubicle, her movements jerky and agitated. She kept running a hand through her already messy hair, her breath coming in shallow gasps. The memory was a physical weight in her chest—the sticky sweetness of melted popsicles and the relentless, echoing shouts of the counselors. She stopped abruptly, whirling around to face Ben, who was calmly sipping his coffee. "We were there," she stated, her voice dangerously low, each word edged with palpable tension. "The same camp. I can practically taste the stale graham crackers right now." Ben lowered his mug slowly, his expression unreadable. "Oh? That’s interesting, Maria. Did you have a good time?" The casual tone struck a nerve. Maria’s hands balled into fists at her sides, and she let out a short, humorless sound that was halfway between a sigh and a growl. "Good? It was chaos. And I wish we could erase that whole experience."
